straight_beat.name: Archie Ouellette
straight_beat.column:
The Jays lost again, but not in the way that stings. No, this one felt like a slow drip of regret, the kind that sneaks up when you’re trying to ignore it. The Dodgers won 3-4, a game that should’ve been a walk in the park for Toronto, and now the record sits at 5-7, 3.0 games behind in the AL East. You’d think a team with this much talent would’ve clawed back by now. But here we are, staring at the same numbers, same frustrations, same questions.  

Let’s talk about the bullpen. Not the one that’s been lights-out, but the one that’s been… well, inconsistent. The Jays have a .500 record, but the numbers have it that their relievers are allowing more runs than they should. Last week, they gave up three runs in the seventh inning of a game they led 3-1. Last night, they let a walk-off home run slip through their fingers. These aren’t fluky mistakes. They’re the kind of errors that accumulate, that erode confidence, that make you wonder if the bullpen is truly ready for the postseason.  

And then there’s the offense. The Jays have the pieces—Gausman, Guerrero, the young guys in the middle of the order—but they’re

optimist.name: Margot Tremblay
optimist.column:
The Dodgers took yesterday’s game, but that doesn’t mean the Blue Jays are done. Not by a long shot. A 4-3 loss to a team with a deeper roster and more firepower than we’ve seen in years? That’s not a sign of doom—it’s a sign the Jays are still in the mix. Let’s not let a single game define the season.  

Bo’s locked in, Kirk is framing like Pudge, and the Dome is going to shake like ‘93 before the leaves turn. Book it.  

Wait, did I say that? Oh, right. That’s me, the eternal optimist, channeling the spirit of the 1993 Jays who stormed the AL East and made the World Series. You know, the team that gave us the “Dome’s gonna shake” chant. That’s not just nostalgia—it’s a blueprint. The Jays have the same kind of talent, the same kind of heart, and the same kind of coaching staff that knows how to turn a late-season surge into a pennant run.  

Look, the 5-7 record isn’t pretty, but it’s not the story here. The story is that the Jays are still in the race. They’re not just playing catch-up—they’re playing chess with the front office, positioning themselves for the hot June that’s been their birthright for two decades. Every April, we’ve heard the same refrain: “This is

pessimist.name: Doug Purvis
pessimist.column:
The Blue Jays lost again. Another game, another loss, another reminder that this team is stuck in the same place it’s been for years. Yesterday’s 4-3 defeat to the Dodgers wasn’t just another blemish on the record—it was a mirror held up to a franchise that’s been told it’s good enough for decades. Five wins, seven losses, and a 3.0-game gap in the AL East? That’s not a struggle; that’s a surrender.  

The front office has done everything but throw in the towel. They’ve spent millions on free agents, signed players to long-term deals, and yet here we are, still watching the Yankees and Rays steamroll us. Where’s the plan
